items.q: Why is 1688 called the Glorious Revolution?
items.correct: Parliament invited William of Orange to replace the Catholic James II, with little bloodshed in England, establishing parliamentary supremacy and the 1689 Bill of Rights.
items.misconception: It was glorious because no one died anywhere in the conflict.
items.random_wrong: William of Orange was Charles II's son and inherited the throne.
items.why: It was bloody in Ireland and Scotland (Boyne, Killiecrankie). Students take the English-centric label literally and miss the violent imposition of the new regime elsewhere.

items.q: Why did the Industrial Revolution begin in Britain?
items.correct: A combination of capital from trade and empire, coal and iron deposits, agricultural improvements, political stability, and a culture of invention.
items.misconception: Because James Watt invented the steam engine.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain banned slavery before other countries.
items.why: Inventions are effects of the conditions, not causes. Steam engines existed before Watt; what mattered was the economic and social environment that made them profitable to deploy at scale.

items.q: What happened in the Russian Revolution of 1917?
items.correct: There were two revolutions: February overthrew the Tsar and installed the Provisional Government; October saw the Bolsheviks seize power from that government.
items.misconception: The Bolsheviks overthrew the Tsar in 1917.
items.random_wrong: Lenin personally led the storming of the Winter Palace.
items.why: Students collapse 1917 into one event. The Tsar abdicated in February under pressure from liberals and soldiers — the Bolsheviks took power eight months later from a republican government.

items.q: In which year did William of Orange land at Torbay?
items.why: It's 1688. “1685” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Bill of Rights. “Act of Settlement” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bishops were prosecuted by James II in June 1688?
items.why: The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
